elements.sent-2411-001: Reinforcement Learning from Human feedback (RLHF) has become a powerful tool to fine-tune or train agentic machine learning models.
elements.sent-2411-002: Similar to how humans interact in social contexts, we can use many types of feedback to communicate our preferences, intentions, and knowledge to an RL agent.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, applications of human feedback in RL are often limited in scope and disregard human factors.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we bridge the gap between machine learning and human-computer interaction efforts by developing a shared understanding of human feedback in interactive learning scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-005: We first introduce a taxonomy of feedback types for reward-based learning from human feedback based on nine key dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our taxonomy allows for unifying human-centered, interface-centered, and model-centered aspects.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition, we identify seven quality metrics of human feedback influencing both the human ability to express feedback and the agent’s ability to learn from the feedback.
elements.sent-2411-008: Based on the feedback taxonomy and quality criteria, we derive requirements and design choices for systems learning from human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-009: We relate these requirements and design choices to existing work in interactive machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-010: In the process, we identify gaps in existing work and future research opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-011: We call for interdisciplinary collaboration to harness the full potential of reinforcement learning with data-driven co-adaptive modeling and varied interaction mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-012: For different types of feedback, we also need to consider human-centered, interface-centered and model-centered qualities which influence how feedback can be collected and processed.. \end{teaserfigure.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The development of intelligent agents that can execute tasks, align with human preferences, and adhere to our values represents a significant long-term goal in artificial intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-014: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and related approaches have emerged as promising tools for training such agents, shaping them to follow instructions and align with user preferences~\cite{christiano2017deep, ibarz2018reward, ouyang2022training .}
elements.sent-2411-015: While research on training RL systems with human input has a rich history—including approaches like imitation learning~\cite{hussein2018 , inverse RL~\cite{ng1999policy , and interactive RL~\cite{cruz2021interactive — RLHF has recently gained prominence due to its success in fine-tuning language models~\cite{ouyang2022training .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Past research has explored diverse interaction modalities for human feedback, including demonstrations, evaluative and comparative feedback, and corrections.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, the field lacks a common classification of design dimensions for human feedback and a unified perspective that bridges human-computer interaction, machine learning, and explainable AI.
elements.sent-2411-018: Human feedback in real-world scenarios encompasses a wide array of communication strategies adapted to various contexts, tasks, and recipients~\cite{cherry1966human .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In contrast, feedback commonly used in RLHF, in particular for the fine-tuning of generative models such as LLM, i.e., pairwise comparisons~\cite{christiano2017deep, wirth2017survey of outputs, represents just a very limited way to provide feedback.}
elements.sent-2411-020: We therefore argue that the space for possible feedback in human-AI communication should be broadened.
elements.sent-2411-021: This paper aims to address two key research questions:.
elements.sent-2411-022: What different kinds of human feedback exist?.
elements.sent-2411-024: To answer the first question, we propose a taxonomy that structures human feedback along nine dimensions, linking these to the broad spectrum of existing work in human-AI communication.
elements.sent-2411-025: For the second question, we discuss seven quality metrics to evaluate the effectiveness of human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-026: The paradigm shift towards more expressive, targeted, and dynamic human feedback enables a flexible, multi-faceted training process for intelligent agents.
elements.sent-2411-027: In this new paradigm, humans interact with agents at various stages of training and deployment, while agents learn from diverse data sources, environments, and feedback types.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our contribution is threefold: (i)~We introduce a taxonomy of nine dimensions of human feedback towards AI agents, unifying model-based, interface-centered, and user-centered aspects.
elements.sent-2411-029: This taxonomy provides a foundation for classifying existing feedback patterns and includes a formalization of human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-030: We relate these to existing work across interactive machine learning and visual analytics, identifying research opportunities for dynamic multi-type human feedback.

elements.section-2411-003: Mapping out the Space of Human Feedback in Reinforcement Learning
elements.sent-2411-087: While existing work in human-in-the-loop RL raises some crucial points, it needs an exhaustive look at feedback types, conflating different concepts and levels.
elements.sent-2411-088: This section proposes a novel, exhaustive taxonomy of human feedback types.
elements.sent-2411-089: In the following sections, we discuss feedback quality criteria and derive actionable requirements from an algorithmic and visual analytics perspective.
elements.subsection-2411-003: A Conceptual Framework of Human Feedback
elements.sent-2411-090: Let us revisit our running example.
elements.sent-2411-091: In a social context, we intuitively use various methods to provide feedback to a household robot, such as commenting on a specific action ("You put the object where I wanted to"), comparing to other behavior ("Your previous trial was better than this one"), stating a goal ("I want the table to be covered with a green tablecloth"), or even via non-verbal interactions like gestures of approval or disapproval.
elements.sent-2411-092: As designers of Human-AI communication spaces, we face two main challenges: (1) designing expressive user interactions, including in non-physical learning environments, and (2) ensuring the given feedback is learnable by the agent, i.e., can be interpreted as a reward function by an RL agent.
elements.sent-2411-093: Structuring the feedback space is crucial for RL and interactive machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-094: We propose a conceptual framework to structure and unify the space of different feedback mechanisms in related work and guide future exploration and research.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/Survey_Methodology.png} \caption{\label{fig:survey_methodology} The methodology used to create the conceptual framework: During the initialization, we extract a series of initial dimensions and qualities that exhaustively classify a set of highly relevant surveyed papers. In a second refinement phase, we iteratively refine the conceptual framework based on additional papers, external input, and internal discussions. Importantly, the framework is also implemented as a software system and validated to handle multiple use cases. Finally, we validate the refined conceptual framework via the implementation and in expert interviews. }
elements.sent-2411-095: We designed the conceptual framework through an iterative survey and refinement process.
elements.sent-2411-096: In multiple iterations, we surveyed work on possible types of human feedback for training intelligent agents and then discussed potential dimensions for classifying human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-097: Discussions were conducted in a core group of four contributors, with an additional open-group session involving six experts.
elements.sent-2411-098: Based on these discussions, we defined dimensions and quality criteria.
elements.sent-2411-099: For the chosen dimensions and quality criteria, we specified the following requirements:.
elements.sent-2411-100: Exhaustiveness: All surveyed papers describing types of human feedback for agent training must be classifiable with the given dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-101: Orthogonality: Significantly different approaches should be classified into separate dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-102: Relevance: The chosen dimensions should directly relate to reinforcement learning-like scenarios, i.e., for user interface design or reward modeling.
elements.sent-2411-103: We began with a limited survey of 18 papers on natural language feedback.
elements.sent-2411-104: We identified five initial types of human feedback (equivalent to the types specified in~\autoref{subsec:types_of_feedback ) and defined a first candidate set of possible dimensions to classify feedback types.}
elements.sent-2411-105: We then extended the candidate set with an additional 22 papers describing approaches for human feedback in reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-106: We defined a set of 6 dimensions and seven quality criteria to classify the surveyed papers accurately.
elements.sent-2411-107: Finally, based on a keyword search, we performed an exhaustive survey of papers in relevant adjacent fields.
elements.sent-2411-108: We searched for relevant papers matching the following keywords: Human Feedback Reinforcement Learning, Human-In-The-Loop Reinforcement Learning, Interactive Reinforcement Learning, Human-Agent Interaction, and Agent Machine Teaching.
elements.sent-2411-110: For the final survey, 141 papers were surveyed in journals and conferences, mainly AAAI, AAMAS, ACL, CHI, CoRL, HAI, HRI, IJCAI, ICRA, NeurIPS.
elements.sent-2411-111: We restricted the search to papers describing possible human feedback in human-agent interactions.
elements.sent-2411-112: To identify dimensions for future research, we did not limit ourselves to papers from core RLHF, i.e., learning a reward model from human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-113: Instead, we considered a wide range of approaches utilizing feedback in human-AI interaction.
elements.sent-2411-114: Based on the final survey, we decided on nine dimensions and seven quality criteria summarized from the surveyed literature.
elements.sent-2411-115: Beyond a systematic review, the taxonomy of feedback types serves two main purposes: (1) We can specify a set of model- and human-centered qualities that must be considered when using human feedback in a system (\autoref{sec:qualities ).}
elements.sent-2411-116: The goal of this chapter is to build upon previous efforts to formalize the notion of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) \cite{christiano2017deep, kaufmann2023survey, casper2023open , in order to combine perspectives from machine learning and human-computer interaction.}

A feedback instance is based on a target and measured values. It is translated into a form that can be used to update a reward model. The policy of the agent, in turn, is optimized with respect to the updated reward model.} \label{fig:feedback_process}
elements.sent-2411-117: As in traditional reinforcement learning (RL), we consider an agent that acts in an environment over a sequence of steps.
elements.sent-2411-118: Concretely, at a timestep $t$, the agent chooses an action $a^t \in \mathcal{A \subseteq \mathbb{R ^M$ given an observation $o^t \in \mathcal{O}$ of its current state $s^t \in \mathcal{S \subseteq \mathbb{R ^N$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Here, we assumed, without the loss of generality, that we can map all actions $a^t$ and states $s^t$ to a real-valued vector.
elements.sent-2411-120: Contrary to traditional RL, however, we lack access to the true \footnote{In many scenarios, a true reward function might not exist, especially if the desired output can only be judged based on subjective, potentially inconsistent, preferences, such as aesthetic criteria {reward function $r: \mathcal{S \times \mathcal{A \to \mathbb{R}$, that provides a reward $r^t = r(s^t, a^t)$ for a state-action pair.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: In many real-world problems, defining a valid reward function is as hard as solving the problem itself.
elements.sent-2411-122: Instead, RLHF resorts to learning an estimate $\hat{r : \mathcal{S \times \mathcal{A}$ of the true reward function based on human preferences transmitted via human feedback.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: The goal is to learn an optimal policy $\pi^\ast: s^t \to a^t$ that maximizes the expected discounted rewards according to $\hat{r}$.

elements.sent-2411-128: In this paper, we assume human feedback to be based on a measurement $m$, obtained in the human-AI interaction process.
elements.sent-2411-129: A measurement is defined for a set of measurement variables $m := {v_1, ..., v_k}$, which depend on the design of the human-AI interaction interface.
elements.sent-2411-130: Variables can be based on diverse interactions, including explicit inputs like buttons or sliders and sensors measuring implicit interactions.
elements.sent-2411-131: Language feedback is a versatile type of feedback which can be interpreted in different ways.
elements.sent-2411-132: Any state information can be interpreted as a measurement as well.
elements.sent-2411-133: We may consider a measurement as a "snapshot" of the interaction process that can be arbitrarily complex based on the chosen analysis context—for example, a "goal state" like a desired arrangement of objects on shelve.
elements.sent-2411-134: Based on the Human-AI communication space~\autoref{subsec:problem_characterization , we define a feedback state $fs$ in which the interaction takes place and that a measurement is conditioned on.}

elements.sent-2411-306: We discuss how these seven qualities can be evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-307: We also discuss how we can measure each quality, i.e., which metrics exist.
elements.sent-2411-308: From a human perspective, we identify two key desiderata with their associated qualities: feedback should be expressive, and it should require minimal effort to provide.
elements.sent-2411-309: These qualities directly relate to the human-centered dimensions of \humanul{intent \refhuman{D1 , \humanul{expression form \refhuman{D2 , and \humanul{engagement \refhuman{D3 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-310: Expressive feedback effectively conveys a human's intent, with implicit feedback potentially carrying richer expressive information than explicit feedback.
elements.sent-2411-311: Implicit feedback typically requires less effort than explicit feedback, and engagement significantly influences the human's perceived effort.
elements.sent-2411-312: Users should be able to provide open \humanul{implicit feedback when needed.}
elements.sent-2411-313: As a human-centered quality, expressiveness is evaluated through human-centered evaluation methods \cite{iml_review_sperrle .. Metrics:.}
elements.sent-2411-314: Satisfaction: Users can rate their satisfaction with the feedback's expressiveness during the interaction process~\cite{koppol2023interactive .}
elements.sent-2411-315: Chosen Feedback: When multiple feedback types are available, users' choices can reveal their preferences~\cite{metz2023rlhf .}
elements.sent-2411-316: These choices often indicate feedback appropriateness, though care should be taken to minimize confounding factors to obtain meaningful preference data.
elements.sent-2411-317: Engagement Metrics: Retention rates, frequency of additional user comments, and similar behavioral indicators can serve as proxy measures for perceived expressiveness~\cite{koppol2023interactive .}
elements.sent-2411-318: Optimizing expressiveness: To optimize expressiveness, users must be able to effectively communicate their intent in any given state.
elements.sent-2411-319: We hypothesize that instructive feedback (particularly demonstrations) will be perceived as more expressive, especially when agents exhibit low skill levels.
elements.sent-2411-320: Additionally, open-ended implicit or multi-modal feedback options may enhance the user's sense of expressiveness.
elements.sent-2411-321: We will discuss further research opportunities in~\autoref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-322: In contrast, \humanul{explicit feedback generally demands higher cognitive engagement, especially for demonstrations and descriptions.}
elements.sent-2411-323: The field of human-computer interaction offers numerous experimental tools for measuring users' cognitive workload~\cite{kosch2023survey, iml_review_sperrle .. Metrics:.}
elements.sent-2411-324: Cognitive Load: This can be assessed through surveys or ratings that measure experienced cognitive demand and exhaustion~\cite{kaufmann2023survey, koppol2023interactive .}
elements.sent-2411-325: Regular assessments during the interaction process can track exhaustion levels.
elements.sent-2411-326: Response time measurements can serve as proxy indicators for cognitive demand, particularly when analyzing how interaction times evolve throughout the process~\cite{scherf2022learning .}
elements.sent-2411-327: Temporal Demand: This metric quantifies the time required to provide feedback~\cite{bignold2023human .}
elements.sent-2411-328: Temporal demand typically correlates with cognitive load~\cite{bignold2021evaluation and has significant implications for budgetary efficiency~\cite{fachantidis2017learning .}}
elements.sent-2411-329: Optimizing ease: User experience can be enhanced by aligning feedback mechanisms with human intent, leveraging implicit feedback where appropriate, and striking an optimal balance between proactive and reactive feedback approaches.
elements.sent-2411-330: From an interface or system perspective, good feedback needs to fulfill two criteria mainly: It should be definite, i.e., a faithful representation of the human expression and its associated uncertainty, and it ideally has a tractable context, i.e., the context necessary to correctly interpret the context is available and transparent.
elements.sent-2411-331: Alternatively, the influence of external context should be minimized.
elements.sent-2411-332: We distinguish between \humanul{user uncertainty , \combul{systematic uncertainty , and \modelul{model uncertainty .}}}
elements.sent-2411-333: We define user uncertainty as something that the user is either aware of (\humanul{explicit ) and could quantify or which is communicated via \humanul{implicit interactions, e.g., hesitation or correcting feedback (\humanul{implicit ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-334: Systematic uncertainty is inherent in the system; we might also refer to it as aleotoric or irreducible uncertainty of the reward learning process.
elements.sent-2411-335: Lastly, model uncertainty is the uncertainty of the reward model concerning human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-336: This uncertainty has a reducible and irreducible component: Some part of it, the epistemic uncertainty of the reward learning process, is reducible by acquiring more data about the human via feedback.
elements.sent-2411-337: However, the inherent uncertainty from the human and systematic uncertainty can not be reduced with more information.
elements.sent-2411-338: Measurement Complexity: Which measurement variables are tracked (i.e., how well defined the feedback state $f_s$ is) and whether uncertainties, inherent to the user or introduced in the measurement process (i.e., when translating implicit feedback), are quantified.
elements.sent-2411-339: Completeness: As part of the feedback measurement process, tracking a wide set of user interactions (such as mouse events, timings, interaction with visual interactions, etc.) enables more comprehensive modeling~\cite{faulkner2020interactive, bignold2021evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-340: Sensor Uncertainty: In particular for implicit feedback, the quality of feedback might rely on the quality of sensor reading~\cite{cruz2018multi .}
elements.sent-2411-341: User Uncertainty: As part of the feedback process, users might be able to quantify their uncertainty for a given target (e.g., by a slider or by expressing uncertainty in natural language).
elements.sent-2411-342: We might also estimate uncertainty based on additional contextual measurements.
elements.sent-2411-343: Feedback with a high level of definiteness enables the system to accurately model the feedback state and uncertainty associated with a feedback instance.
elements.sent-2411-344: Optimizing definiteness: Measuring additional metrics beyond the explicit feedback interactions, including contextual factors like expertise level, background, and task familiarity, improves definiteness.
elements.sent-2411-345: Importantly, inputs to quantify uncertainty by the user or measuring system help to model feedback more accurately.
elements.sent-2411-346: On the other hand, external context dependency encompasses factors that are not explicitly modeled as part of the analysis context.
elements.sent-2411-347: For example, \combul{hypothetical absolute feedback, e.g., demonstrations, are often generated \humanul{implicitly in response to the current state of training, e.g., a desire to instruct agent behavior based on recent observation.}}
elements.sent-2411-348: Similarly, isolated ratings of episodes might also be influenced by previous internal contexts.
elements.sent-2411-349: In \combul{relative feedback, we provide a reference as a controlled internal context.}
elements.sent-2411-350: External context, which encompasses, e.g., the cultural or sociological context of the human and the surrounding of the interaction environment, can influence how certain types of feedback are communicated.
elements.sent-2411-351: In general, the less formalized feedback, e.g., \combul{hypothetical \humanul{descriptive and \humanul{implicit feedback, the higher the degree of external context-dependency can be expected.}}}
elements.sent-2411-352: Gestures, mimics, or natural language expressions may vary significantly between humans, whereas external factors influence simple scores or comparisons less.
elements.sent-2411-353: These allow us to relate the feedback and identify both confounding factors and whether the feedback is robust to these.. Metrics:.
elements.sent-2411-354: Knowledge Dependency: Feedback may be heavily dependent on the user's knowledge (both in terms of global domain knowledge or the knowledge during a given moment of the training).
elements.sent-2411-355: Tracking the knowledge of a user when measuring a feedback instance allows us to measure these dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-356: Design Dependency: Feedback values depend on design choices in the implementation of the feedback process, e.g., choice set dependency~\cite{freedman2021choice ("From how many different choices can a user select?").}
elements.sent-2411-357: Thus, measuring the effects of design variations on human feedback allows us to model these effects.
elements.sent-2411-358: Feedback Interdependency: Feedback may depend on previous feedback instances, i.e., the magnitude of values is influenced by previous values~\cite{li2021learning .}
elements.sent-2411-359: One must treat sequential feedback as interdependent instead of viewing each instance as isolated to measure this bias.
elements.sent-2411-360: Policy Dependency: Feedback depends not only on the agent behavior and task but also the current policy of an agent~\cite{macglashan2017interactive .}
elements.sent-2411-361: In particular, humans adapt their feedback to the policy they observe during training.
elements.sent-2411-362: Measuring feedback values during the training process can uncover these dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-363: Optimizing context independence: From an interface perspective, feedback is more independent of external context if it contains internal contextual information, i.e., relative feedback already induces a context between multiple targets, whereas absolute feedback is potentially more context-dependent (this comparative stability of relative (preference) feedback is generally accepted in the literature \cite{wirth2017survey ).}

elements.sent-2411-366: Two qualities can be derived directly from machine learning fundamentals: Assuming there exists an internal, i.e., not directly observable, human reward model \footnote{There is extensive discussion why this assumption is not generally valid \cite{casper2023open , we want the feedback to have low variance/high precision and low bias, i.e., representing the human internal rewards accurately.}}
elements.sent-2411-367: Additionally, we want feedback to be informative, i.e., revealing as much as possible about the internal human reward model.

elements.section-2411-005: Designing Systems for Diverse Human Feedback: Existing Approaches and Opportunities
elements.sent-2411-411: In the previous sections, we have presented a conceptual framework of nine dimensions, as well as seven qualities for human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-412: Now, we want to outline how we can operationalize the framework.
elements.sent-2411-413: We begin by briefly introducing a basic architecture, i.e., the main components of a system for reinforcement learning from human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-414: Then, we outline requirements and design choices for systems targeted at human feedback spanning the space of dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-415: We reference existing work in human-AI interaction and interactive RL for each dimension.
elements.sent-2411-416: Additionally, the framework allows us to identify research gaps and opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-417: In \autoref{fig:basic_system_design , we summarize the identified core components of an RL-based human-AI interaction setup: A \humanul{user interface , implementing feedback interactions, as well as providing a view of the two-sided communication, i.e., the targets feedback can be provided for and potentially additional information about the agent or training process.}}
elements.sent-2411-418: The \combul{feedback processor takes the data recorded in feedback interactions, potentially unstructured and highly diverse, and translates it into a standard format that can be passed onto the reward modeling stage.}
elements.sent-2411-419: The \modelul{reward model is optimized based on human feedback and must capture human preferences accurately and allow the AI agent to optimize its behavior to maximize the predicted reward.}
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=0.65\linewidth]{figs/basic_system_design_v2.png} \caption{\label{fig:basic_system_design} The basic system components for reward learning: A \humanul{user interface}, \combul{feedback processor}, and \modelul{reward model}. }
elements.sent-2411-420: The choice of feedback generally influences the whole stack of system components, i.e., implementing feedback of different types requires adaptations to the UI, feedback processor, and reward model.
elements.sent-2411-421: In the following chapter, we discuss the influence of feedback on all system components.
elements.sent-2411-422: How can humans communicate with the agent? Depending on the setting, we have specific interaction mechanics, like verbalization, movement, or using a GUI.
elements.sent-2411-423: Interactive visual user interfaces can enable expressive and situation-aware multi-type feedback.
elements.sent-2411-424: The user interface is responsible for recording measurements, intrinsic and contextual ones.
elements.sent-2411-425: Therefore, an ideal interface might go beyond just implementing the narrow explicit interactions and provide additional inputs, different types of feedback, and even multi-modal inputs like camera, voice, etc. \requirementbox{User Interface Requirements {HumanColor {The user interface serves as the interface for communication between the human and the agent.}}}
elements.sent-2411-426: Here, the human inputs feedback via interactions and receives information about the system and the environment.
elements.sent-2411-427: We infer the user interface requirements on human-centered qualities $\refhuman{Q1}$, $\refhuman{Q2}$, as well as interface-centered qualities $\reffbp{Q3}$, $\reffbp{Q4}$.
elements.sent-2411-428: However, UI design has huge implications for the reward model training as well: {(UI.R1) Give users the ability to express feedback matching their intentions.}
elements.sent-2411-429: Implement versatile input interactions for evaluative, instructive, or descriptive feedback should be available.
elements.sent-2411-430: When going beyond simple, explicit feedback, which can be fed to a reward model directly, we must integrate a processing step that translates the measurements into "learnable" inputs for the reward model.
elements.sent-2411-431: The feedback processor implements the translation algorithm $\phi$ specified above.

elements.sent-2411-433: The processor also serves as the query engine, which handles target selection $(\mathcal{T \sim T)$, i.e., determines which targets are shown to the human user.}
elements.sent-2411-434: This querying can be combined with varying levels of human involvement, i.e., manual or assisted selection of targets by the human user.
elements.sent-2411-435: The processor has access to all relevant data in the analysis context and mediates bi-directional processes such as querying and user-aware modeling.
elements.sent-2411-436: We identify the following requirements, considering dimensions, based on qualities \reffbp{Q3 , \reffbp{Q4 , \refmodel{Q5 , \refmodel{Q6 , and \refmodel{Q7 : {(FP.R1) Define a consistent protocol to translate different forms of human feedback to a format that can be passed onto a reward model.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-437: As introduced in \autoref{subsec:problem_characterization , the RL-based learner needs access to a reward function that assigns state-action(-state) pairs(triplets) a scalar-valued reward.}
elements.sent-2411-438: This reward function can be implemented in a variety of ways: It can be explicitly supplied by the environment, e.g., by being implemented as a table stating the reward for a fixed set of state-action trajectories~\cite{Sutton2018 or an in-game score~\cite{Bellemare2013 .}}
elements.sent-2411-439: However, this means the reward function is fixed or pre-specified.
elements.sent-2411-440: A reward model is a dynamic reward function that can be updated, e.g., with human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-441: A reward model is used to train the agent asynchronously.
elements.sent-2411-442: Because a model can predict reward for arbitrary state-action sequences, asking for human feedback after each step or episode is unnecessary.
elements.sent-2411-443: The choice of function class determines the expressiveness of the reward model, i.e., the complexity of factors that can be considered.
elements.sent-2411-444: Here, we identify a trade-off between different models: We may use complex high-fidelity models that can capture a large degree of variation and potential nuance, e.g., by modeling the human as a separate agent with a partially observable state~\cite{hadfield2016cooperative, lindner2021learning, shah2020benefits .}
elements.sent-2411-445: However, such models may require large amounts of data to provide ``learnable'' reward estimation, particularly in complex scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-446: On the other hand, we can choose simpler models that cannot capture certain aspects, e.g., fatigue or updating knowledge in humans.

elements.sent-2411-449: When building systems for multi-type human feedback, a crucial factor influencing design is the task/scenario and, more broadly, the task domain.
elements.sent-2411-450: We designed the conceptual framework to be task-agnostic, i.e., applicable to any scenario.
elements.sent-2411-451: However, as the task heavily influences feedback interaction design, we want to discuss task considerations briefly.
elements.sent-2411-452: Specifically, we want to highlight different task categories.
elements.sent-2411-453: An agents attempt at a task can be objectively or subjectively evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-454: We may also call certain tasks verifiable or non-verifiable.
elements.sent-2411-455: Objective tasks, such as mathematical calculations, with a clear objective solution or measurable outcome, are a good fit for automated validation strategies and require less or no human evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-456: Here, human feedback might be better suited as a source to give feedback on the process or intermediate steps, i.e., descriptive or instructive.
elements.sent-2411-457: Closely related to the previous point, tasks may be either open-ended, i.e., not having a clearly defined outcome (e.g., "Write a story..") or close-ended ("put the dirty dishes into the dishwasher").
elements.sent-2411-458: Open-ended tasks are well served by expressive, diverse feedback to give users ways to express complex preferences.
elements.sent-2411-459: Simple, explicit feedback can be the right choice for close-ended, clearly defined tasks.
elements.sent-2411-460: A key consideration is the level of knowledge expected to give feedback for a task.
elements.sent-2411-461: Tasks might be either highly domain-specific, requiring rare expert knowledge, or be domain-general, i.e., can be evaluated or even solved by large parts of the population.
elements.sent-2411-462: We must collect feedback from domain experts for domain-specific tasks, e.g., coding tasks.
elements.sent-2411-463: Consequently, we both must be efficient with feedback, i.e., focusing on highly informative feedback and giving domain experts ways to express their knowledge by providing interactions for instructive or descriptive feedback.
elements.sent-2411-464: For domain-general tasks, simplicity of interactions and cost-effectiveness might be more important considerations.
elements.sent-2411-465: As for feedback, the solution for a given task might be highly context-dependent, or context-independent.
elements.sent-2411-466: In context-dependent tasks, correct agent behavior must be adapted to specific circumstances.
elements.sent-2411-467: For example, some tasks must consider a user's background, adapt behavior, and interpret feedback accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-468: Based on this, we may adapt the feedback to track contextual factors or ignore them during translation and modeling.
elements.sent-2411-469: We can think of many additional task categories, which all influence the choice of feedback interactions.
elements.sent-2411-470: Based on our presented conceptual framework, we settle with the call to consider adaptive and task-specific human feedback.

elements.subsection-2411-017: Systems for Feedback of Different Interface-Centered Dimensions
elements.sent-2411-543: We want to discuss proposed solutions and opportunities for feedback on different relations, content levels, and actuality.
elements.sent-2411-544: Compared to the previous dimension, the choice of interface-centered dimensions is more dependent on the actual type of target (i.e., text, images, numerical values), so we will comment on this difference when appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-545: The move from absolute to relative feedback, i.e., from approaches like interactive shaping to preference-based RL, has been established over the last few years.
elements.sent-2411-546: Preference-based RL is now the standard RLHF approach, particularly for fine-tuning LLMs \cite{Ziegler2019finetuning, ouyang2022training .}
elements.sent-2411-547: Binary preferences strike a favorable combination of simplicity, which is especially relevant for crowd-sourced feedback and stability of feedback.
elements.sent-2411-548: So far, relative feedback, such as preferences or rankings, has been used almost exclusively for evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-549: Here, they are easily implemented, e.g., via simple button interfaces, and work for modalities that can be visualized (text, but also videos of execution).
elements.sent-2411-550: However, some work has, e.g., investigated the use of implicit feedback methods to extract relative feedback.
elements.sent-2411-551: So far, relative feedback for instructions or descriptions has been investigated to a far smaller degree.
elements.sent-2411-552: Here, a potential example would be action advice with multiple actions (i.e., recommending a best, but also second-, third-best action, etc.).
elements.sent-2411-553: Similarly, users could be asked first to generate a few demonstrations and then rank them according to self-perceived quality, resulting in relative demonstrative feedback.
elements.sent-2411-554: Similarly, it might be possible to formulate relative descriptive statements or rules instead of absolute constraints or descriptive statements.. \oppbox{4.}
elements.sent-2411-555: Target Relation {As relative feedback often has significant advantages in terms of context dependency \reffbp{Q4 , a currently under-explored research direction is the use of instructive/descriptive feedback as relative feedback, e.g., by giving humans the ability to express preferences between demonstrations or descriptions like rules and constraints.}}
elements.sent-2411-556: By varying contextual feedback for the same target, we can use relative relationships between measurements to define influencing factors \reffbp{Q3 .}
elements.sent-2411-557: A critical dimension to improving feedback's expressiveness is using feature-level feedback.
elements.sent-2411-558: Instance-level is the most dominant type of feedback because it can be implemented via simple user interactions (like sliders, buttons, mimics detection \cite{knox2009interactively, li2018interactive, christiano2017deep ).}
elements.sent-2411-559: Even though user interactions for demonstrative feedback are generally more challenging to implement, instance-based feedback requires uni-directional interactions that let human users select or generate actions.
elements.sent-2411-560: On the other hand, feature-level feedback requires more complex user interfaces in order to let users select parts of the feature space (e.g., selecting regions of image-based input as salient features \cite{metz2023rlhf, zhang2019leveraging ).}
elements.sent-2411-561: Some feature-level feedback might be collected via implicit, non-conscious interactions such as gaze tracking~\cite{zhang2020atari .}
elements.sent-2411-562: However, we might collect such feedback via explicit annotations, i.e., by allowing a human user to annotate behavior and states, selecting sub-features of the generated state-action sequences~\cite{zhang2023self .}
elements.sent-2411-563: The differentiation between instance- and feature-level feedback for language models is more challenging because we could interpret each predicted token as an action of the agentic language model.
elements.sent-2411-564: Therefore, annotating single words from a fully generated text could be interpreted as fine-grained feature-level feedback~\cite{wu2023fine .}
elements.sent-2411-565: However, a better interpretation of feature-level feedback in the context of natural language might be the annotation of specific facts or generation patterns that occur over multiple generations (i.e., are not bound to a specific generated instance).
elements.sent-2411-566: User interfaces allowing these feature-level annotations might open novel ways to train language models.
elements.sent-2411-567: Finally, meta-level feedback is an emerging technique in RLHF.
elements.sent-2411-568: Such feedback can be integrated into the normal interaction process, either before a session or during normal feedback interactions.
elements.sent-2411-569: One example is skill assessment questions~\cite{singhal2024scalable , which uses additional contextual questions not directly related to feedback to asses the domain knowledge of a human labeler.}
elements.sent-2411-570: This, in turn, allows us to calibrate the expected quality of reward for certain difficult queries.
elements.sent-2411-571: For such queries, feedback from domain experts should be rated higher than feedback from a lay user.
elements.sent-2411-572: A second example is distinguishability queries~\cite{feng2024comparing , which combines pairwise preferences with queries to measure which segment pairs are easy or difficult to distinguish for labelers.}
elements.sent-2411-573: In turn, we can trust preference pairs more highly distinguishable than pairs that are only hard to differentiate.
elements.sent-2411-574: We can also adopt our querying strategy to serve pairs specifically.. \oppbox{5.}
elements.sent-2411-575: Content Level {In our mind, moving beyond instance-level feedback is a key step towards more expressive feedback.}

elements.sent-2411-577: Exploring ways to collect, encode, and process this feedback is crucial for more powerful human-AI communication.
elements.sent-2411-578: Meta-level feedback can be intensively valuable in understanding contextual factors of human feedback and calibrating the reward learning process accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-579: Combining instance-level feedback with content-level annotations and meta-level interactions is a way to combine learnability \refmodel{Q5}\refmodel{Q6}\refmodel{Q7 with expressiveness \refhuman{Q1 , improving definiteness of feedback \reffbp{Q3 and context dependence~\reffbp{Q4 .}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-018: Systems for Feedback of Different Model-Centered Dimensions
elements.sent-2411-594: Finally, we want to discuss proposed solutions and opportunities for feedback on different granularities, choice set sizes, and exclusivity.
elements.sent-2411-595: An element missing from simple approaches, e.g., in ranking-based reward learning~\cite{Ziegler2019finetuning , is a way to get a good overview of the candidate set, i.e., the set of possible instances available for feedback.}
elements.sent-2411-596: For inspiration, we can look at existing visual interactive labeling approaches~\cite{bernard2018vial, bernard_towards_2018, bernard2018vial that describe the unification of labeling across machine learning and visual analytics.}
elements.sent-2411-597: The VIAL process introduces certain important steps, which we also find for reward-based learning from human feedback: (1) Visualizing a model and its progress during training.
elements.sent-2411-598: Multiple works have explored visual interactive labeling, e.g., integrating gamification and explainable AI~\cite{sevastjanova_questioncomb_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-599: Instead of instances where we want to assign a discrete or numeric label, we are interested in assigning rewards to state-action sequences of varying lengths.
elements.sent-2411-600: Compared to data types such as images, state-action sequences can have a high degree of heterogeneity, particularly due to different lengths and content.
elements.sent-2411-601: We examine existing approaches to visualizing state-action sequences in interactive machine-learning systems.
elements.sent-2411-602: As previously outlined, these instances can include states, observations, actions, scalar quantities such as cumulative reward, and sequences thereof, for example, state-action sequences or more abstract objects like policies.
elements.sent-2411-603: We can differentiate between two main approaches to state visualizations.
elements.sent-2411-604: In the first, states are visualized directly, and in the second, the visualization of the state is a function of the state itself, which applies further processing or abstraction.
elements.sent-2411-605: Many popular reinforcement learning environments already provide visual representations of states that could convey the agent's state without further processing.
elements.sent-2411-606: In other domains, however, visualizing states directly is not possible.
elements.sent-2411-607: Instead, the state is shown by creating a 3D rendering of the robot in its environment.
elements.sent-2411-608: Actions are often not visualized directly but are left implicit and need to be inferred by the user by comparing consecutive visualizations of states, which might be reasonable for small and discrete action spaces but becomes infeasible for large or continuous spaces, where the size of the action space does not permit this approach.
elements.sent-2411-609: The user interface provides a summary of chosen, discrete action distributions per episode as a pie chart and the temporal progression of these action distributions throughout training as a stacked bar chart.
elements.sent-2411-610: States, chosen actions, and resulting states are highly interdependent, and as a result, existing visualizations often display them jointly.
elements.sent-2411-611: A widely-used approach to visualizing state-action sequences is to show them as videos, where each frame corresponds to the visualization of a single state in the sequence~\cite{Atrey2020Exploratory, McGregor2015Facilitating .}
elements.sent-2411-612: As alluded to earlier, this approach has its limitations, as it does not explicitly show the actions taken by the agent.
elements.sent-2411-613: The color of the marker encodes one of three actions, either left, right, or idle.
elements.sent-2411-614: Similar approaches have been used for continuous action spaces, e.g., in Verma et al.~\cite{verma2019programmatically , which plotted continuous actions against time steps.}
elements.sent-2411-615: While visualizing the state or observation of an agent provides an intuitive understanding of single states, understanding the distribution of such states and their relationship to each other is challenging due to their often high-dimensional nature.
elements.sent-2411-616: Dimensionality reduction methods have been used to project state-action pairs into a low-dimensional latent space, providing an overview of a state-action distribution found, e.g., in replay buffers~\cite{vizarel .}
elements.sent-2411-617: The authors suggest that RL users can obtain a quick overview of the state-action diversity of an agent.
elements.sent-2411-618: Instead of visualizing the artifacts generated by policies, e.g., state-action distributions as described before, some authors have attempted to provide visual summaries of policies directly.

elements.sent-2411-619: Temporal Granularity {While many approaches for single granularities exist, there is still a gap in the simultaneous visualization of state-action sequences, which restricts effective exploration and selection by humans.}
elements.sent-2411-620: While the ability to give feedback at different granularities also positively impacts expressiveness \refhuman{Q1 ,.}
elements.sent-2411-621: The available choice set is highly dependent on the task domain, but there are choices a designer can make for different feedback.
elements.sent-2411-622: As with other dimensions, we find a trade-off in the choice set size: A larger choice set is potentially more expressive and informative.
elements.sent-2411-623: Having only a limited set of choices might feel restrictive, i.e., the human user feels like they lack control over the feedback.
elements.sent-2411-624: In terms of information, k-wise ranking compared to binary preferences~\cite{Zhu2023, myers2022learning can be more informative because a k-wise ranking contains multiple binary preferences.}
elements.sent-2411-625: Similarly, the choice of the best action out of a larger set of available actions could be interpreted as ruling out more sub-optimal actions.
elements.sent-2411-626: Conversely, a larger set of possible choices can lead to user fatigue and higher cognitive demand.
elements.sent-2411-627: Additionally, a larger choice set can also lead to a higher variance in the given values, although it might exhibit a larger bias if the given choices do not match the true desired internal best choice.. \oppbox{8.}
elements.sent-2411-628: Choice Set Size { The choice set size is partially dependent on the chosen task, and there are apparent designs based on other chosen dimensions (e.g., for a demonstration, the choice set size is dependent on the action space, i.e., from which set of actions I can choose for the demonstration).}
elements.sent-2411-629: However, there is potential in varying the choice set to find an optimal setup.
elements.sent-2411-630: For example, rankings might have advantages over pairwise comparisons; similarly, instead of generating a novel trajectory in a continuous space, we might want to select from a set of candidate trajectories instead to improve efficiency \refhuman{Q2 .}
elements.sent-2411-631: We might find that a more extensive choice set size might positively influence unbiasedness \refmodel{Q6 , because the human is not forced to provide values in a restricted range.}
elements.sent-2411-632: On the other hand, a large choice set size might lead to a higher variance \refmodel{Q6 .}
elements.sent-2411-633: Finally, a small choice set size might increase learning speed because the model can be simpler, but choosing from a large choice set could be more precise and, therefore, informative \refmodel{Q7 .}
elements.sent-2411-634: A potential remedy is using adaptive choice feedback, which dynamically adapts the choice size during the feedback generation process according to human and model qualities.
elements.sent-2411-635: Training a new reward model for each human, estimating irrationality or the connection between internal reward and observable behavior is infeasible for most learning scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-636: To overcome this, we can draw inspiration from recommender systems that are challenged with the so-called ``cold-start'' problem, i.e., the initialization of models without any data~\cite{wang2022deep, afsar2022reinforcement .}
elements.sent-2411-637: We can utilize meta-learning \defmodel{ML strategies to quickly adapt models to the given use case.}
elements.sent-2411-638: A way to achieve this would be to train population models that, e.g., capture certain feedback types' general irrationality.
elements.sent-2411-639: Based on this model, we could train a user model that captures the particularities of the user, e.g., specific ways implicit feedback is communicated.
elements.sent-2411-640: Finally, we might then use a session or task model that learns a reward function specific to the given task and can also consider session-specific feedback quality.. \oppbox{9.}
elements.sent-2411-641: Exclusivity { Experimenting with the use of multiple feedback sources is a promising avenue.}
elements.sent-2411-642: When dealing with feedback from multiple human labels, as for large-scale post-training of LLMs \cite{ouyang2022training , we are natively confronted with non-exclusive reward sources and potential problems like labeler disagreement.}
elements.sent-2411-643: This can lead to problems like decreased precision \refhuman{Q5 .}
elements.sent-2411-644: Different reward sources might exhibit different biases (like rating behavior based on different internal scales) or different preferences.
elements.sent-2411-645: Modeling different non-exclusive reward sources directly might help to alleviate some issues, actually correcting individual human biases \refhuman{Q6 , and detecting the quality of sources in terms of informativeness \refmodel{Q7 .}}
elements.sent-2411-646: The opportunities for human-centered human feedback are summarized in~\autoref{fig:model_opp_summary .}
elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figs/model_opportunities_summary.png} \caption{A Summary of Opportunities for Model-Centered Dimensions} \label{fig:model_opp_summary}
elements.sent-2411-647: The goal of training with tight human-AI interaction is to create powerful, aligned, and trustworthy agents.
elements.sent-2411-648: Considering our running example, Our household robot might now be able to perform various additional tasks, act according to our usual preferences, and be predictable and reliable in its movements and actions.
elements.sent-2411-649: As we have outlined in this paper, we think that diverse and complementary feedback types are essential for humans to give expressive feedback, require appropriate effort, enable reasoning about contextual information and uncertainty, and are learnable and informative.
elements.sent-2411-650: Powerful and natural human-AI interactions may only be achieved if diverse interactions and feedback types are blended dynamically and flexibly.
elements.sent-2411-651: Thus, human-AI communication incorporating multiple types of human feedback, expressive reward modeling, querying, and translation are part of a conceptual model for the next steps in RL with human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-652: Future work across different areas can contribute towards the common goal of human-aligned intelligent agents:.
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=.95\linewidth]{figs/Teaser_Figure.png} \caption{\label{fig:prototype} Prototype implementation of an integrated system for multi-type human feedback in reinforcement learning. (A) is a scrollable list of episodes: The user can be queried for feedback by visual indicators, selected episodes are visible, and the user can visit previous episodes to investigate the effect of feedback. In the center column, we have an episode view with multiple feedback interactions (B): The user evaluates the episodes via ratings (C), ranks via drag \& drop (D), select episode segments for correction (E), or brush to highlight important features (F). Furthermore, the human annotator is informed of global model training via output metrics (G). Shown is BabyAI~\cite{chevalier-boisvert_babyai_2018}. }
elements.sent-2411-653: By using different interactions for feedback, humans have more ways to express their goals and preferences to AI systems, which can improve safety, robustness, and satisfaction on the users' side.
elements.sent-2411-654: Researchers with HCI or visualization backgrounds can contribute systems to evaluate the utility of different feedback types.
elements.sent-2411-655: In \autoref{fig:prototype , we show a prototype of a possible interface integrating several types of feedback into a common interface.}
elements.sent-2411-656: To achieve this, these models need to receive additional information as input, for example, user and session information, potentially model temporal dynamics, and need the capacity to learn the irrationality or uncertainty of different feedback types.
elements.sent-2411-657: Meta-learning approaches can support adapting expressive models to new users and keep the demand for data and calibration in check.
elements.sent-2411-658: Instead, we should ground such considerations on resulting modeling decisions on rigorous experimentation with human subjects across diverse backgrounds and tasks~\cite{iml_review_sperrle .}
elements.sent-2411-659: Versatile visual interactive systems that allow for experimentation can be crucial tools to enable this experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-660: We find a critical current research gap in investigating the trade-off between natural language feedback, which was proposed in recent papers~\cite{harrison2017guiding, sumers_linguistic_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-661: However, as has been argued in the space of explainable AI~\cite{sevastjanova2018going , natural language has its limit, and we might instead view other modalities as complementary.}
elements.sent-2411-662: For example, language alone can make it difficult to refer, e.g., to a certain granularity, feature, etc. \end{siderules The given conceptual framework should enable thinking about human feedback in a structured and systematic way and inspire future system design for expressive, efficient, and highly effective learning of reward models.}
elements.sent-2411-663: However, there are many learning factors from human feedback that are still not covered, e.g., temporal dynamics, or this framework does not fully discuss co-adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-664: Furthermore, although we developed the framework in an iterative process, there might be other ways to frame the problem space for different use cases.
elements.sent-2411-665: This paper discusses a conceptual framework for human feedback for reinforcement learning in human-AI collaboration.
elements.sent-2411-666: We have presented nine dimensions to classify feedback types, encompassing human-centered, interface-centered, and model-centered dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-667: Additionally, we have presented seven quality criteria for human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-668: We outline the design requirements for interactive systems using reinforcement learning from human feedback for the three core components.
elements.sent-2411-669: We argue that systems using a rich set of possible feedback with expressive reward models, supported by targeted visual interactive interfaces, can lead toward human-aligned intelligent agents.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format .}

elements.sent-2411-677: The development of intelligent agents that can execute tasks, align with human values, and adhere to our preferences represents a significant long-term goal in artificial intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-678: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and related approaches have emerged as promising tools for training agents, shaping them to follow instructions and align with user preferences~\cite{christiano2017deep, ibarz2018reward, ouyang2022training .}
elements.sent-2411-679: While research on training intelligent agents with human input has a rich history—including approaches like imitation learning~\cite{hussein2018 , inverse RL~\cite{ng1999policy , and interactive RL~\cite{cruz2021interactive — RLHF specifically has gained prominence due to its success in fine-tuning language models~\cite{ouyang2022training .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-680: However, the field lacks a common classification of design dimensions for human feedback and a unified perspective that bridges human-computer interaction and machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-681: In this new paradigm, humans may interact with agents at various stages of training and deployment while agents learn from diverse data sources, environments, and feedback types.
elements.sent-2411-682: Our contribution is threefold: (i)~We introduce a taxonomy of nine dimensions of human feedback towards AI agents, unifying model-centered, interface-centered, and user-centered aspects.
elements.sent-2411-683: Throughout the paper, we will use two running examples: Due to its ubiquity in current RLHF research~\cite{ouyang2022training, bai2022constitutionalaiharmlessnessai, kaufmann2023survey ; we will use the example of human feedback for a text generation model and outline potential feedback in such a use case.}
elements.sent-2411-684: Especially beyond simple, verifiable, and single-objective generation tasks, including multi-step reasoning or domain-dependent task execution, more expressive feedback can be highly desirable.
elements.sent-2411-685: Additionally, we consider a more advanced, future-oriented use case of a household robot deployed in a new household environment.
elements.sent-2411-686: Human feedback is transmitted from human to agent.
elements.sent-2411-687: Humans benefit from high expressiveness (see~\autoref{sec:qualities ), allowing effective communication of internal knowledge, goals, and preferences.}
elements.sent-2411-688: Traditionally, the research field of interactive machine learning (IML) addresses this direction~\cite{amershi2014power, review_interactive_ml_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-689: In the opposite direction, agents provide machine feedback via model outputs or auxiliary data.
elements.sent-2411-690: High-fidelity outputs accurately describe model behavior~\cite{gunning2019xai , but this information must be comprehensible to humans via abstractions or filtering, which limits fidelity.}
elements.sent-2411-691: Rich, diverse interactions via expressive human feedback can enable the capture of additional contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-692: Pairwise preferences have become so ubiquitous that RLHF is often synonymously with ``RL from human preferences''.
elements.sent-2411-693: While existing work in human-in-the-loop RL raises some crucial points, it does not provide a consistent view of feedback types, conflating different concepts and levels.
elements.sent-2411-694: This section proposes a novel, exhaustive categorization of human feedback types.
elements.sent-2411-695: The following sections discuss feedback quality criteria and derive actionable requirements for human-AI interaction systems.
elements.figure-2411-040: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/Survey_Methodology_v2.png} \caption{\label{fig:survey_methodology} The methodology used to create the conceptual framework: During the initialization, we extract a series of initial dimensions and qualities that exhaustively classify a set of highly relevant surveyed papers. In a second refinement phase, we iteratively refine the conceptual framework based on additional papers, external input, and internal discussions. Importantly, the framework is also implemented as a software system and validated to handle multiple use cases. Finally, we validate the refined conceptual framework via the implementation and in expert interviews. }
elements.sent-2411-696: Relevance: The chosen dimensions should relate to the elements of the human-to-AI communication process and enable deriving quality metrics and requirements.
elements.sent-2411-697: We begin with a limited survey of 18 papers on natural language feedback.
elements.sent-2411-698: Finally, we performed an exhaustive survey of papers in relevant adjacent fields based on a keyword search.
elements.sent-2411-699: For the final survey, 141 papers were surveyed in journals and conferences, including AAAI, AAMAS, ACL, CHI, CoRL, HAI, HRI, IJCAI, ICRA, NeurIPS.
elements.sent-2411-700: We restricted the search to papers describing the use of human feedback in human-agent interactions.
elements.sent-2411-701: Instead, we considered various approaches utilizing feedback in human-AI interaction.
elements.sent-2411-702: For the formalization introduced in the following section, we aimed to find a common abstraction of terms and concepts described in the surveyed work beyond a specific scenario that builds a foundation for the formalization of identified dimensions, Beyond a systematic review, the taxonomy of feedback types serves two primary purposes: (1) We can specify a set of human-, interface- and model-centered qualities that must be considered when using human feedback in a system (\autoref{sec:qualities ).}
elements.sent-2411-703: Before introducing the identified dimensions on a conceptual level, we also want to discuss a formalization of the human feedback space to foster the development of machine learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-704: Therefore, we build upon previous efforts to formalize the notion of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) \cite{christiano2017deep, kaufmann2023survey , in order to combine perspectives from machine learning and human-computer interaction.}
elements.sent-2411-705: Contrary to traditional RL, however, we lack access to the true\footnote{In many scenarios, a true reward function might not exist, especially if the desired output can only be judged based on subjective, potentially inconsistent, preferences, such as aesthetic criteria.}
elements.sent-2411-706: Language feedback is a versatile type of feedback that can be interpreted in different ways.
elements.sent-2411-707: To achieve this, a vector of measurements $m$ must be assigned to a target $\mathcal{T \subseteq \Xi$ from the space of possible trajectories $\Xi$, i.e., all possible behaviors.}
elements.sent-2411-708: In the following sections, we introduce nine dimensions that help to structure the space of possible design choices and help us to think about diverse human feedback in a systematic way.
elements.sent-2411-709: Despite this grouping, the full set of dimensions influences all design and interpretation stages.
elements.figure-2411-041: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/dimensions_figure_v2.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:dimensions} In the context of Reinforcement Learning, we present a conceptual framework of human feedback based on nine dimensions: We identify \humanul{human-centered}, \combul{interface-centered} and \modelul{model-centered} dimension. }
elements.sent-2411-710: While there is ample space for possible intentions in general, we focus on four attributes that are exhaustive in the human-AI context: \humanul{evaluating the agent, i.e., assessing how well it is doing, \humanul{instructing the agent, i.e., communicating a desired behavior or outcome, \humanul{describing , i.e., providing supplementary information to complement other feedback.}}}
elements.sent-2411-711: The following dimensions are relevant when processing human feedback as they determine which information can be encoded.

elements.sent-2411-716: As a human-centered quality, expressiveness is evaluated through human-centered evaluation methods \cite{iml_review_sperrle .. Subcategories:.}
elements.sent-2411-717: Open-Endedness: tasks may be either open-ended, i.e., not having a clearly defined outcome (e.g., "Write a story..") or close-ended ("put the dirty dishes into the dishwasher").
elements.sent-2411-718: Optimizing expressiveness: To optimize expressiveness, users must be able to effectively communicate their \humanul{intent \refhuman{D1 in any given state.}}
elements.sent-2411-719: We hypothesize that \humanul{instructive or \humanul{descriptive feedback will be perceived as more expressive, especially when agents exhibit low skill levels or the human has expert knowledge.}}
elements.sent-2411-720: Additionally, \humanul{open-ended , \humanul{implicit or \humanul{multi-modal feedback \refhuman{D2 options may enhance the user's sense of expressiveness.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-721: Similarly, the opportunity for users to act \humanul{proactively \refhuman{D3 for targets has the potential to increase perceived expressiveness.}}
elements.sent-2411-722: The field of human-computer interaction offers numerous experimental tools for measuring users' cognitive workload~\cite{kosch2023survey, iml_review_sperrle .. Subcategories:.}
elements.sent-2411-723: Knowledge Level: A key consideration is the level of knowledge expected to give feedback for a task.
elements.sent-2411-724: Optimizing ease: User experience can be enhanced by aligning feedback mechanisms with human \humanul{intent \refhuman{D1 , avoiding ineffective or repetitive feedback.}}
elements.sent-2411-725: Leveraging \humanul{implicit feedback \refhuman{D2 where appropriate, and striking an optimal balance between proactive and reactive feedback approaches \refhuman{D3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-726: These qualities can be mainly influenced by choices in the design dimensions of \combul{relation \reffbp{D4 , \combul{content level \reffbp{D5 , and \combul{actuality \reffbp{D6 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-727: Systematic uncertainty is inherent in the system; we refer to it as aleotoric or irreducible uncertainty of the reward learning process.
elements.sent-2411-728: Task Verifiability: An agents attempt at a task can be objectively or subjectively evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-729: Optimizing definiteness: \combul{Relative feedback, or \combul{absolute feedback \reffbp{D4 with ways to communicate confidence/uncertainty generally has higher definiteness.}}}
elements.sent-2411-730: Measuring additional metrics beyond the explicit feedback interactions, including contextual factors like expertise level, background, and task familiarity, improves definiteness.
elements.sent-2411-731: We summarize many of these measurements as \combul{meta-level \reffbp{D5 feedback.}}
elements.sent-2411-732: Finally, feedback for \combul{observed \reffbp{D6 feedback has a significantly higher degree of definiteness because we can directly control the selection and definition of samples.}}
elements.sent-2411-733: On the flip side, \combul{generated feedback might reflect the background and user context in a way that would be hard to uncover with pre-defined targets.}
elements.sent-2411-734: These allow us to relate the feedback and identify both confounding factors and whether the feedback is robust to these.. Subcategories:.
elements.sent-2411-735: Task Dependency: The solution for a given task might be highly context-dependent, or context-independent.
elements.sent-2411-736: User Dependency: Feedback may be heavily dependent on the user's knowledge (both in terms of global domain knowledge or the knowledge during a given moment of the training).
elements.sent-2411-737: Interface Dependency: Feedback values depend on design choices in the implementation of the feedback process, e.g., choice set dependency~\cite{freedman2021choice ("From how many different choices can a user select?").}
elements.sent-2411-738: Model Dependency: Feedback depends not only on the agent behavior and task but also the current policy of an agent~\cite{macglashan2017interactive .}
elements.sent-2411-739: Optimizing context independence: From an interface perspective, feedback is more independent of external context if it contains internal contextual information, i.e., \combul{relative \reffbp{D4 feedback already induces a context between multiple targets, whereas \combul{absolute feedback is potentially more context-dependent (this comparative stability of relative (preference) feedback is generally accepted in the literature \cite{wirth2017survey ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-740: However, other contextual factors might influence feedback, particularly if it is formulated more openly \cite{lindner_humans_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-741: Similar to the previous quality, additional measurements (meta feedback \reffbp{D5 ) can be used to determine dependencies better.}
elements.sent-2411-742: Enabling users to provide \combul{generated \reffbp{D6 feedback beyond actually \combul{observed targets or choices also allows them to learn more about context dependencies, as the choice of generated feedback by the human user can also reveal contextual information.}}}
elements.sent-2411-743: Model-centered qualities should be evaluated quantitatively by analyzing the feedback values and model performance.
elements.sent-2411-744: The observed variance might serve as an approximate measure of overall feedback variance.. Subcategories:.
elements.sent-2411-745: Translation Accuracy: Translating implicit feedback might introduce additional variance into feedback values, e.g., if the translation is also based on probabilistic modeling~\cite{cruz2018multi .}
elements.sent-2411-746: Secondly, reducing the \modelul{choice set \refmodel{D8 might improve precision/consistency because choices are more distinct.}}
elements.sent-2411-747: Although multiple \modelul{non-exclusive reward sources~\refmodel{D9 have advantages (see the following quality criteria), they might introduce additional variance, as different sources potentially disagree with each other.}}
elements.sent-2411-748: T counteract the increased variance, the total number of samples must be increased.
elements.sent-2411-749: We can specify a reference set of pre-labeled target data (in experimental settings, we could use ground-truth reward data) and then compare the human feedback with this reference data to estimate reward bias.
elements.sent-2411-750: In future cases, we can apply these learned bias estimates to new human feedback.. Subcategories:.
elements.sent-2411-751: Distribution Bias: A simple bias that has been described in the literature is asymmetry bias, specifically humans tending to give more positive than negative feedback for rating-based feedback \cite{thomaz2007asymmetric, knox2012reinforcement .}
elements.sent-2411-752: The pretense of individual biases, e.g., in questionnaires~\cite{choi2004catalog , is long-established.}
elements.sent-2411-753: We should, therefore, expect such distributional biases for evaluative feedback in RLHF.
elements.sent-2411-754: Contextual Bias: As mentioned previously (see~\reffbp{Q4 ), the context can introduce biases in feedback.}
elements.sent-2411-755: The context can act as a confounding factor in feedback.
elements.sent-2411-756: By defining the context, we can measure its effect on the feedback \cite{bignold2021evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-757: Complexity Bias: A specific type of bias, prominently observed for LLMs is a bias towards more complex, e.g., lengthy or expertly seeming outputs or behaviors \cite{singhal2023long, park2024disentangling, hosking2024human .}
elements.sent-2411-758: Temporal Bias: Finally, feedback in a particular situation can be biased by temporal dependencies, e.g., by other proceeding targets, ordering of targets, or learning/adaption processes during the interaction \cite{li2021learning, saito2023verbositybiaspreferencelabeling .}
elements.sent-2411-759: Humans might give different feedback for the same target at different stages of the human-AI process.
elements.sent-2411-760: In general, using a combination of \modelul{non-exclusive reward sources~\refmodel{D9 , e.g., from different intentions, granularities~\refmodel{D7 or choice set sizes~\refmodel{D8 can be used to correct for specific biases of single sources.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-761: Optimizing informativeness: Based on metrics such as uncertainty or expected information gain, we can actively query the user for targets and feedback types~\cite{jeon_reward-rational_2020, ghosal2022effect, zhan2023query .}
elements.sent-2411-762: Being able to adapt \modelul{granularity ~\refmodel{D7 or \modelul{choice set size ~\refmodel{D8 of a query improves the possibility of receiving targeted and varied feedback.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-763: Access to multiple \modelul{non-exclusive reward sources~\refmodel{D9 also opens up the space for informative queries and feedback.}}

elements.sent-2411-765: We begin by briefly introducing a basic architecture, i.e., the main components of a system learning from expressive reinforcement learning from human feedback.

elements.figure-2411-047: \definecolor{blueish}{HTML}{F4F7F8} \rowcolors{2}{white}{blueish} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{1.5pt} \renewcommand\arraystretch{1.20} \begin{tabular}{r | p{10cm}} Publication Description Kuhlmann et al.\cite{kuhlmann2004guiding} State-dependent if-then-else rules to guide agent Tenorio-Gonzalez et al.\cite{tenorio2010dynamic} Using language commands (e.g., action to take, evaluation, confirmation of reached goal state) Krening et al.\cite{krening2018newtonian} Language instructions mapped to actions in a grid world domain Krening et al.\cite{krening2018interaction} Language instructions/binary critique for grid world domain De Winter et al.~\cite{de2019accelerating} Verbally telling constraints to the system in natural language, then translated into first-order logic; input can be temporal action sequences Bignold et al.~\cite{bignold2021persistent} When giving a rating for a state, the user can also formulate a general rule; multiple rules are processed with probabilistic policy reuse Sumers et al.\cite{sumers2021learning} Simple game-like object collecting example; linguistic feedback is grouped into three categories and produced for observed episodes Okudo et al.\cite{okudo2021subgoal} Generate subgoals for a more complex overall behavior in a grid-world domain (points to reach via a UI drawing style); for a robot (verbal description of subgoals to reach) Millan-Arias et al.~\cite{millan2021robust} No interactive experiment discussed; using simulated oracles for cartpole language-based actions Chi et al.\cite{chi2022instruct} Humans are queried to either teach via instruction/action advice or evaluation on a five-point scale for predicted action; simplified medical care setting Van Waveren et al.\cite{van2022correct} From natural language and "block-based" (i.e., programming language) feedback, infer action refinement, alternative actions, and forbidden actions Bai et al.\cite{bai2022training} Chat interface; ranking of two entries with confidence (one or the other is better) Trick et al.\cite{trick2022interactive} Giving action advice (encoding by mentioning an object for the robot to grip) via language (with keyword spotting) or by interpreting pointing gestures; robotics grasping task Lin et al.~\cite{lin2022inferring} Flight booking use case; users can give natural language feedback to an agent; reward function is inferred from free-form feedback Ying et al.\cite{ying2023inferring} First, humans utter an instruction, then perform a series of actions; goal is inferred from both; grid world domain Lukowicz et al.\cite{lukowicz2023sa} Speech and visual cues to penalize/reinforce (anticipated) behavior Liu et al.\cite{liu2023interactive} User interrupts robot behavior to utter verbal corrections \end{tabular} \caption{User Interface: Language}
elements.figure-2411-048: \definecolor{blueish}{HTML}{F4F7F8} \rowcolors{2}{white}{blueish} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{1.5pt} \renewcommand\arraystretch{1.20} \begin{tabular}{r | p{11cm}} Publication Description Yu et al.\cite{yu2023language} Robotics domain; language is translated into rewards; demonstrated as a chat-like interface where humans can give multiple text feedback utterances, e.g., comments on quality, what to do instead/focus on Wu et al.\cite{wu2023fine} Feedback for text; interactive text annotations on sentence-granularity, separate annotations for missing information, relevance, and factuality Yow et al.~\cite{yow2024extract} Robot object grasping scenarios; correct trajectories of robots in space via language corrections (e.g., move closer to, go down, etc.) \end{tabular} \caption{User Interface: Language (continuation)}
